gene_description: Carboxylesterase 1 — primary enzyme responsible for hydrolysis and metabolism of methylphenidate in the liver. Loss-of-function variants lead to significantly elevated drug exposure.

source.title: The impact of CES1 genotypes on the pharmacokinetics of methylphenidate in healthy Danish subjects
source.finding: G143E (rs71647871) carriers showed 149% increase in d-methylphenidate AUC (2.5-fold higher exposure), and required significantly lower doses to achieve clinical response.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the G143E loss-of-function variant. Markedly decreased CES1 enzymatic activity leading to dramatically elevated methylphenidate plasma levels. Expected >4-fold increase in drug exposure. Extremely rare genotype but carries high risk of adverse effects including cardiovascular events, insomnia, and appetite suppression.
AA.recommendation: Use significantly reduced methylphenidate dose (consider ≤25% of standard dose) or avoid methylphenidate entirely. Consider non-CES1-metabolized alternatives such as amphetamine-based stimulants, atomoxetine, or guanfacine. Closely monitor blood pressure and heart rate.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the G143E variant. CES1 activity is reduced, resulting in approximately 2.5-fold higher d-methylphenidate exposure (149% increase in AUC). Clinical responders with this genotype required significantly lower doses than wild-type individuals.
AG.recommendation: Start methylphenidate at a lower dose (consider 50% of standard starting dose) and titrate cautiously. Monitor for dose-dependent side effects including insomnia, appetite loss, tachycardia, and elevated blood pressure. This variant also affects dabigatran metabolism.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the G143E variant. CES1 activity is reduced, resulting in approximately 2.5-fold higher d-methylphenidate exposure (149% increase in AUC). Clinical responders with this genotype required significantly lower doses than wild-type individuals.
GA.recommendation: Start methylphenidate at a lower dose (consider 50% of standard starting dose) and titrate cautiously. Monitor for dose-dependent side effects including insomnia, appetite loss, tachycardia, and elevated blood pressure. This variant also affects dabigatran metabolism.

GG.effect: Wild-type CES1 function. Normal methylphenidate hydrolysis and clearance. Standard pharmacokinetic profile expected.
GG.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing per clinical guidelines. No pharmacogenomic dose adjustment needed.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. Associated with moderately reduced CES1 activity and increased methylphenidate exposure. Also relevant for dabigatran metabolism, where this variant has been associated with reduced bleeding risk due to lower active drug levels.
CC.recommendation: Monitor for enhanced methylphenidate effects. Consider dose reduction if side effects are prominent. For dabigatran, this variant may provide a modest protective effect against bleeding.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Slight reduction in CES1 activity with marginally increased drug exposure for CES1 substrates.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor clinical response and adjust as needed.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Slight reduction in CES1 activity with marginally increased drug exposure for CES1 substrates.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor clinical response and adjust as needed.

TT.effect: Wild-type alleles. Normal CES1 enzymatic function and standard drug metabolism.
